26.1.label: Question 26.1
26.1.question:
Explain the possible consequences of the failure of a reinsurer for a health and care insurance
   company.

Solution: If the insurer does not use the failed reinsurer, there would probably be no direct consequences.
However, even then, the availability or cost of reinsurance in the future may suffer.                    [½]
The rest of this answer assumes that the insurer did use the reinsurer.
The insurer will be liable for the reinsurer’s share of claims that would otherwise have been
recoverable.                                                                                             [½]
This will not only be for future claims on existing business, but may also affect reported claims in
payment, for which recoveries may no longer be able to be made.                                    [½]
The insurer may have to pay a greater share of many claims, in the case of proportional
arrangements, ...                                                                                        [½]
... or may now be liable for very large claims in the event of non‐proportional arrangements.            [½]
This could have a serious adverse impact on the financial stability of the insurer, …                    [½]
… which might result in premium rate increases.                                                          [½]
In addition, the statutory solvency position of the insurer may worsen.                                  [½]
The insurer should therefore attempt to replace the reinsurance for its in‐force policies that are
not claiming, to prevent being exposed to large new claims from that business.                    [½]
It would then only be losing the unexpired reinsurance premium for these policies.                       [½]
The insurer will also have to review its reinsurance arrangements for new business by seeking
cover elsewhere.                                                                              [½]
Both of these arrangements may now, however, prove to be expensive.                                 [½]
[Maximum 4]

26.2.label: Question 26.2
26.2.question:
Describe the facultative and treaty methods of reinsurance, listing the advantages and
    disadvantages of the two methods.

Facultative: Facultative reinsurance is an individual arrangement where an insurer approaches a reinsurer to
reinsure part of a particular risk.                                                           [½]
There is no obligation on the insurer to offer business and no obligation on the reinsurer to agree
Advantages and disadvantages: +         It provides greater flexibility, so the arrangement that is most suitable for the insurer can
+         It can be used to cope with risks that fall outside the terms of a treaty, eg those that are too
large or have unusual risk characteristics.                                                   [½]
–         It needs to be arranged for each risk, and so can involve extra work and expense in setting
–         The insurer may not be able to find suitable reinsurance.                                       [½]
–         There can be a delay for the insurer in finding cover before accepting the risk.                [½]
+         It gives the insurer the certainty of being able to have reinsurance cover, so it can do its
financial and risk planning with greater certainty …                                         [½]
… and be confident that it will be able to offer products, so this helps marketability.         [½]
+         It can be simple to operate (eg quota share), and so involve less expense.                      [½]
+         It can help the insurer control its solvency and growth requirements.                           [½]
–         It takes time / expense to set up in the first place.                                           [½]
–         The treaty terms may restrict the insurer’s freedom.                                            [½]
–         The insurer may not be able to get the best reinsurance deals, especially for large or unusual
Treaty: A treaty is a formal arrangement between an insurer and a reinsurer.                                      [½]
The treaty will specify the terms of the reinsurance, eg types of contract, type of reinsurance,
Treaties are usually arranged so that the insurer is obliged to pass on some of the risk in a defined
However, there may be facultative / obligatory agreements, in which the insurer is not obliged to
The reinsurer is required to accept risks offered that fall within the treaty conditions.                 [½]

26.3.label: Question 26.3
26.3.question: List twenty items that might be included in a reinsurance treaty.

Solution: Depending on the type of reinsurance contract, the details set out in the treaty will include some
of the following:
 reinsurance commissions payabl:          profit commission payable to the insurer and the method of calculation
 claim notification arrangement:          claim payment arrangements, including special arrangements for large claims

26.4.label: Question 26.4
26.4.scenario:
You are the actuary to a reinsurance company. A health insurer with whom you currently have no

involvement has asked for a quote on reinsuring a new stand‐alone guaranteed premium CI
policy.
26.4.question:
Outline the factors you would take into account in proposing suitable risk premium reinsurance
terms.

Solution: The rates offered will depend on the expected claims rate.                                            [½]
The expected claims rate would be estimated by looking at experience with this type of contract
ceded by other insurers.                                                                      [½]
The extent to which the underlying contract differs from that offered by other insurers,
eg whether the same CI definitions are used, would be considered.                                     [½]
Any available industry data may be considered.                                                        [½]
Allowance should be made for expected future trends in CI experience and consideration given to
any uncertainty around this.                                                                [½]
If the reinsurer is particularly unsure about experience, it could offer rates that are reviewable
every year rather than guaranteed rates (the fact that the insurer’s rates are guaranteed does not
mean that the reinsurer’s rates should be guaranteed).                                             [½]
An important thing to check up on is the insurer’s intended underwriting procedure.                   [½]
Being a stand‐alone protection contract, there is a great possibility of selection against the
insurer.                                                                                              [½]
The reinsurer would want to be sure that the insurer will minimise such selection.                    [½]
The intended limits of cover obtainable for the varying degrees of medical evidence should be
considered, ie whether they are satisfactory given the reinsurer’s experience of this contract. [½]
Also, consideration should be given to how the contract is to be marketed and sold and whether
this will affect the expected claims experience.                                             [½]
If the reinsurer is unhappy about any of the underwriting / marketing aspects then it could make
its terms conditional on their being modified.                                                 [½]
The reinsurer could perhaps only offer cover on a proportion of risk above the insurer’s retention,
eg by offering to accept only 75% of risk above the retention (instead of the normal 100%), the
insurer will retain a stronger interest in underwriting quality.                                [½]
The reinsurer could consider a maximum sum insured that it will accept on an ‘obligatory’ basis,
and make anything above that ‘facultative’.                                                    [½]
For this contract, mortality rates are also relevant. These should be based on what the reinsurer
expects, given its experience with similar contracts.                                           [½]
Given the reinsurer’s estimate of the pure premium rates appropriate, margins should be added
for expenses ...                                                                            [½]
... and profit in order to achieve target profitability on the reinsurer’s risk discount rate.            [½]
The loading for expenses might take into account the use of any technical assistance.                     [½]
The resultant risk premium should be compared with other reinsurers’ rates.                               [½]
If the reinsurer particularly wants this business, it might be prepared to lower its rates, contingent
on at least covering the pure risk premium and marginal expenses.                                  [½]
If the insurer wants any financing, then that could be incorporated into the treaty.                      [½]
This arrangement might increase the reinsurer’s profitability, or could help offset low profits from
the risk cover if the premiums are low for competitive reasons.                                    [½]
[Maximum 8]

26.5.label: Question 26.5
26.5.scenario:
A health insurer writes individual income protection contracts with a deferred period of 3 months.
    All benefits are payable monthly in advance.

On 1 January 2018 the insurer purchased reinsurance cover under the following treaty terms:
         Quota share – 75% of each risk is ceded.
         Maximum retention – £1,000 benefit per annum per life.
         All risk prior to 1 January 2018 is to be retained by the insurer.
         Risk premium rates – Reinsurance rates are applied monthly to the sum at risk. The rate
definition is ‘age next birthday at the previous policy anniversary’.

You are given the following extract of the premium rate table:
Age next            Annual rate per £1,000 annual benefit
39                                 1.2

## 40                                 1.3

## 41                                 1.4

## 42                                 1.5

On 1 January 2018 the insurer has 1000 policies aged 40 next birthday under the above rate
definition with the following sum insured profile for each policy:
Number of policies              Sum at risk for each policy
800                    £3,000 annual benefit

## 150                    £4,000 annual benefit

## 50                     £5,000 annual benefit

Reinsurance sum insured: There is a quota share arrangement whereby 75% of each risk is ceded (to the reinsurer), but
there is also a maximum retention of £1,000 benefit pa per life.
The insurer will retain 25% of each risk under the reinsurance arrangement. However, for policies
where the retention exceeds £1,000 pa, this maximum retention will apply, with the excess being
Hence, each of the 50 policies that have sums at risk of £5,000 will have a reinsurance sum

(ii).question:
Assuming that 10% of the policies lapse uniformly during the year, estimate the
reinsurance premium for 2018, stating any assumptions you use.                               [3]

You are given the following extract from the insurer’s claims records:

Claim           Date first sick         Date claim notified     Date claim           Date of recovery            Annual
accepted                                         Benefit
A               5 November 2017         12 December 2017        5 January 2018       4 June 2018                 £3,000

B               12 December 2017        17 January 2018         26 January 2018      11 July 2018                £4,000

C               7 January 2018          18 February 2018        9 March 2018         6 August 2018               £3,000

D               25 June 2018            13 August 2018          18 August 2018       24 August 2018              £3,000

E               21 November 2018        6 January 2019          12 January 2019      20 March 2019               £3,000

Estimated reinsurance premium: Lapses are 10% over the year and are uniform, so 95% of the original SAR will apply during the
At the start of the year, the policies are all aged 40 next birthday at the previous policy
anniversary, and so the premium rate of 1.3 per mille will apply then. During the year, all policies
will have a policy anniversary, and so by the end of the year age 41 next birthday rates (ie 1.4 per
Assuming that policy anniversaries occur uniformly throughout the year, but allowing also for the
fact that policies are lapsing during the year, the insurer would expect slightly more policies to be
charged the 40 next birthday rate compared to the 41 next birthday rate. (This is because there is
more business in force towards the start of the year.)                                             [½]
A crude way of allowing for this is to assume that all policy anniversaries occur half way through
the year. This would mean that all policies in force during the first half of the year are priced at
40nb rates (when between 100% and 95% of the original policies are in force), whereas those in
force during the second half of the year are priced at the 41nb rates (when between 95% and 90%
of the original policies are in force).
Remembering that premiums are expressed as per £1,000 of annual benefit, the reinsurance
This calculation also assumes that:          there are no changes in the SAR during the year, eg new business is ignored                [½]
         reinsurance premiums are still paid on policies that have claims in payment                [½]
         the 10% lapse rate applies equally to all sizes of policy.                            [½]
An alternative, simpler, approach is to calculate the average premium rate applying during the
year. As in the above approach, policy anniversaries are assumed to occur uniformly throughout
the year, however, a straight average of the 40 and 41 next birthday premium rates might be
used. The average premium rate is therefore taken to be 1.35 per mille, giving a reinsurance
### 0.95  2,450  1.35  £3,142: The other assumptions mentioned in the first approach are also required here.

(iii).question:
State, with reasons, whether the reinsurer was required to make payments for each of the
above claims.

Claims for which the reinsurer is requir: Claims A/B: No payment required as sickness commenced prior to 1 January 2018.                       [½]
Claim C: Payment required – sickness commenced during the ‘on risk’ period and sickness lasted
for at least the duration of the deferred period (3 months).                                 [½]
Claim D: No payment required because recovery was made before expiry of the deferred period.
Claim E: Payment required – sickness commenced during the ‘on risk’ period (even though it
wasn’t notified until the following year) and sickness lasted for at least the duration of the

(iv).question:
Calculate the total amount of the claim payments made by the reinsurer in respect of
these claims.

Claim C: Claim commenced (after 3 month deferred period):           7 April 2018
12: So the total claims paid is 750.00  187.50  £937.50                                                 [½]
Claim E: Claim commenced (after 3 month deferred period):           21 February 2019

(v).question:
State the other factors the reinsurer would need to consider in order to determine the
profitability of this business.

Other factors to consider in determining:          the existence of any IBNR claims                                                              [½]
         the reinsurer’s expenses, including commissions payable to brokers                            [½]
         investment returns and timing of payments                                                     [½]
         supervisory solvency requirements, including the cost of any guarantees                       [½]
         the impact of any profit‐sharing agreement between insurer and reinsurer                        [½]
         the reinsurer’s profit criteria / required return on capital                                    [½]
         any reinsured new business during the year                                                      [½]
         any differences from the assumptions made (eg in lapse rates)                              [½]

(i).question:
Describe the aim and main features of excess of loss reinsurance from the point of view of
               a ceding health and care insurance company.

Excess of loss: The aim is to help protect the insurer from large adverse fluctuations in claim costs, in order to
help stabilise its profit flow and ultimately to protect it from ruin.                             [1]
It also helps the insurer to write individual risks that could lead to large claims.                       [½]
Excess of loss reinsurance comes in thre:          aggregate excess of loss / stop loss                                                             [¼]
Risk excess of loss: This operates on an individual policy basis, for any kind of policy that has uncertain claim amounts
Claim amounts in excess of a specified loss level (the excess point) are covered by the
reinsurance, up to an agreed maximum amount.                                                              [½]
Alternatively, only a proportion of the claim above the excess point may be payable by the
The excess point may be index linked, to reflect the expected inflation of claim costs.                    [½]
Aggregate excess of loss / stop loss: This reinsurance extends cover to all causes or all events during the year.                               [½]
Aggregate excess of loss / stop loss does this by covering the total losses for the whole account,
above an agreed limit, for a certain period (usually a year).                                     [½]
The whole account can be one or several classes of insurance.                                              [½]
The excess point and upper limit are often expressed as a percentage of the insurer’s premium
income for that account, ie as a claims ratio.                                                [½]
Claims payable under the contract would be assessed after any other individual reinsurance
(eg risk excess of loss) recoveries had been made.                                                        [½]
Unlike risk excess of loss, this kind of cover can apply to fixed benefit insurances – such as cash
plans or CI insurance – as well as to variable benefit contracts like PMI.                          [½]
This kind of cover is especially important for group health insurance, ...                              [½]
... where the existence of many employees in the same workplace can lead to an increased
incidence of large aggregate claims due to non‐independence of some of the individual health
Long‐term insurance claims are rarely sufficiently volatile to warrant paying for this protection, so
usually this is just used for short‐term classes of health insurance.                              [½]
Catastrophe excess of loss: This reinsurance covers claims arising from a single event and is likely to be used for very large
... eg where claims from the event could be could be potentially solvency threatening, or
All types: The reinsurance premium would need to be renewed and renegotiated on a yearly basis.   [½]

Coinsurance: Coinsurance is the general term given to a PMI policy condition whereby the policyholder is
required to pay, at least in part, for medical expenses incurred, maybe on a percentage basis. [1]
In other contexts, coinsurance also refers to the situation where two or more insurers share the
It also may refer to reinsurance of long‐term business on an original terms basis.                    [½]

26.7.label: Question 26.7
26.7.scenario:
A PMI insurer has the following reinsurance cover:
              a 20% quota share (QS) treaty with Reinsurer X
           an excess of loss (XL) reinsurance treaty whereby Reinsurer Y meets claim amounts falling
between £½m and £1¼m on the insurer’s net account
           a further XL reinsurance treaty whereby Reinsurer Z meets claim amounts exceeding Y’s
upper limit on the insurer’s net account.

(i).question:
Calculate the amounts paid by each of the four parties following a gross claim for £2m. [2]

The PMI insurer’s situation in part (i) is repeated except that the two XL reinsurance treaties now
operate on the original gross claim, and the QS treaty operates on the insurer’s residual net
account after Reinsurers Y and Z have accepted their shares of the risk.

Amount paid by each party (scenario 1): Reinsurer X takes 20% of all claims. Therefore Reinsurer X pays 0.2  £2m = £0.4m.                      [½]
The net claim after QS, which is £1.6m (ie 2  0.4), is over Reinsurer Y’s band of cover. Hence
The insurer has to pay the slice below Reinsurer Y’s band of £0.5m.                                     [½]
This leaves £0.35m to be paid by Reinsurer Z (ie 1.6 – 1.25).                                         [½]

(ii).question:
Determine how much each party will now pay.                                                 [2]

The situation in part (ii) is repeated except that the two XL treaties have been drawn up to include
a stability clause, which has resulted in all the limits increasing by 8% pa compound to the date
the claim is paid.

(iii).question:
Determine how much each party will now pay if the claim amount is again £2m, paid nine
years after the treaty was established.

Amounts paid by each party (scenario 3): The calculations can be simplified because (1.08)9 almost exactly equals 2. Therefore Reinsurer Y’s
Hence, following the same logic in part (ii), the insurer and Reinsurer X share the claim below
Reinsurer Y’s level of cover in the proportion 80:20, ie the insurer pays £0.8m and X pays £0.2m.
The total claim is now less than Reinsurer Y’s upper limit, hence Reinsurer Z pays nothing.            [½]
The remainder of the claim is £1m, which is all paid by Reinsurer Y.                                 [½]

(i).question:
List the risks relating to the insurer’s operations that a reinsurer would face if it made stop
loss cover available.

Risks to a stop loss reinsurer:          insurer’s premiums generally under‐priced (eg a competitive market)
 poor premium rating structure:          adverse claims experience (eg large claims or random events)

(ii).question: Given these risks, why would the insurer probably not want stop loss in practice?

Why a insurer would not want to buy stop: The reinsurer would need to charge a very high premium to cover itself against the risks.

(iii).question:
Suggest two broad responses to the risks in part (i) that the insurer may well find
preferable in practice.

Preferable options for the insurer:          set up tight internal controls to ensure good premium rating, underwriting and claims
         buy particular types of reinsurance to guard against specific events (ie buy individual
excess of loss and catastrophe excess of loss as needed).

26.9.label: Question 26.9
26.9.scenario:
A health and care insurer transacting individual IP insurance business is reviewing its reinsurance
   retention levels.
26.9.question: Outline the factors it would take into account in setting revised retention levels.

Solution: The current retention levels will be the starting point. These will only be changed if there is
evidence that this is necessary.                                                                       [½]
A key issue will be the amount of fluctuation in its experience that the insurer finds acceptable.
The lower the acceptable fluctuation, the lower the retention level.                                   [½]
If underwriting is completed at the time of the proposal, then it is an expense for all policies.
Moratorium underwriting is completed at the time of a claim, so it is only done for the proportion
of policies that result in a claim.
From a policyholder point of view, standard exclusions (eg for specific claims) are arguably
clearer, and this approach is also commonly used for short-term insurances (ie PMI).
The rest of this section describes what happens under the full medical underwriting process
and how it can be used to manage risk.
A moratorium is an initial period of blanket exclusion for any pre‐existing conditions, the proposer
not being medically underwritten unless a claim is made. If no further treatment is received for
these conditions within, say, two years, then the exclusions may be lifted.
An exclusion is where the proposer is medically underwritten, and the policy when issued is
endorsed so that any conditions from which the applicant has suffered (and any related
conditions linked to these) are specifically excluded.
The underwriter is attempting to match the risk with the pricing assumptions and ultimately
to ensure that a suitable premium is charged.
The example starting on the next page shows an entry for Asthma that might be found in a PMI
underwriting manual. The medical details are not important. You will notice that the individuals
are classified into five rating groups (types 1 to 5) based on the use of three risk factors
(frequency of bronchodilator use, frequency of steroid use and history of hospitalisations or
emergency treatment). Age and smoking status are also used as rating factors.
The alternative to accepting business at standard rates, ie charging loaded premiums or imposing
special terms, will often cause bad feeling to the applicant and requires additional administration,
and hence expense, for the insurer.
This could be a significant barrier to sales. For example, if brokers are aware that a large majority
of an insurer’s applicants end up being rated, they may not be keen to sell that insurer’s products.
         For the substandard risks, the medical underwriting process will identify the most
suitable approach and premium level for the special terms to be offered.
         Adequate risk classification within the underwriting process will help to ensure that
all risks are rated fairly.
The aim should be to create risk groups that are homogeneous in terms of their expected
claims costs. The variation in claims costs within each group should be smaller than the
variation between the risk groups.
         Medical underwriting will help in ensuring that actual morbidity experience does not
depart too far from that assumed in the pricing of the contracts being sold.
For larger proposals, financial underwriting is also used. This reduces the risk from over-
insurance.
Over‐insurance is an issue for the policyholder when the insurer’s indemnity is limited to a
maximum payment (eg an IP policy where payments are limited to 60% of salary before the
period of sickness). If a policyholder selects benefits much greater than this, then some of the
insurance is ‘wasted’, as there is normally a limit on benefits (a maximum income replacement
ratio – see Chapter 1) to maintain the financial incentive for the policyholder to return to work.
PMI seeks to be an indemnity product. It pays for the actual costs of treatment (sometimes
subject to an overriding maximum), so it is not possible for the policyholder to buy more
insurance than is necessary, at least from one insurance company.
If the level of income rises, then the insurer will not be financially disadvantaged following a
claim, since the benefit actually paid will now be well below the maximum benefit level.
It may, however, be in the policyholders’ interests to tell the insurer of the increase, since they
may want to ensure that their replacement ratio is broadly unchanged (albeit for a higher
premium). Alternatively, the policyholder may be happy with the original level of benefit
purchased if it is still deemed sufficient to ensure all essentials would be met in the event of a
claim.
The formula used to determine the maximum benefit at claim can be determined by
considering the net replacement ratio (post-disability income after tax compared to pre-
disability income after tax) for a variety of personal circumstances of claimants. The aim
would be to determine a maximum benefit formula that results in net replacement ratios that
are always lower than one.
         Generally, pre‐authorisation gives the insurer more control over the claim process. For
example, it may allow it to influence (or force) the choice of treatment provider.
         It will ensure that the claimant is aware of exactly what treatment will and will not be
covered by the policy, and so should reduce the likelihood of disputes.
         It gives the insurer early warning of impending claims. This gives better management
information and should improve the accuracy of reserves held.
         reconciliation checks, ie that the data at the previous investigation plus the business
coming onto the books minus the business going off the books equals the current data
         consistency checks, eg that premiums are consistent with sums insured
(i)       Factors affecting customers’ expectations
         literature, advice and illustrations provided at the point of sale, as well as before / after
the point of sale (eg notices of benefit change, or marketing literature)
The insurer may still be holding relatively high reserves for this policy, reflecting the high expected
claim costs over the final five years of the policy compared to the (now relatively small) remaining
premiums to be paid. All else being equal, therefore, the insurer would be better off if the policy
lapsed, as the reserve would be released into surplus (instead of being needed to pay claims).
However, all is not likely to be equal. In particular, it is more likely that the policyholder who
lapses is in good health rather than in poor health. As a result of this selection, the insurer would
have been likely to have made a profit from the policy, if it had continued in force (by paying five
more years’ worth of premiums and not claiming), and so the insurer is likely to be worse off as a
result of the lapse.
Poor or ambiguous policy wording can result in dissatisfied policyholders, at a time when they
need financial assistance for deteriorating health. In the interests of customer satisfaction and
public relations, it may be beneficial for an insurer to pay the claim even though strictly speaking
it does not have to.
Indeed the courts, or supervisory authorities (such as the financial ombudsman in the UK), may
rule that such claims should be paid, particularly if it can be proved that customer expectations
were that such claims would be paid. In some cases, the insurer may decide to settle claims out
of court, thus avoiding any adverse publicity and/or legal expenses.
         to improve risk classification for individual risks and hence offer more appropriate terms
to applicants
         to improve the monitoring process and hence take appropriate action, eg by spotting
issues that would otherwise have been missed or by spotting trends sooner
         reputational damage, eg concerns over privacy and data protection failures
         policyholders may not welcome being monitored in this way and as a result, sales might
fall and lapses might rise
         as the use of big data develops, the regulations governing it might change, eg restrictions
on its use / fines for any misuse of the data
         the data collected may be inaccurate, incomplete or irrelevant, eg whether fruit bought at
the supermarket has actually been eaten
         whenever complex models are used there is the risk of choosing the wrong model
         the expenses of collecting and analysing the data may outweigh the benefits of the extra
information received
The amount of fluctuation deemed accepta:          the size of the insurer’s free assets: the lower the free assets, the lower the reinsurance
retention will be                                                                          [½]
         the cost of capital: the higher the cost (of using capital to reduce claim fluctuations), the
lower the retention will be                                                                 [½]
         the level and quality of underwriting: the more strict the underwriting, the more certain
future experience should be, and the higher the retention can be                        [½]
         the mix of business, especially the different benefit sizes: the more volatile the business,
the lower the retention will be.                                                           [½]
Sickness experience is especially volatile. The insurer may be cautious when setting levels for this
class.                                                                                           [½]
If the reinsurance is being used wholly or partly to reduce new business strain, then as well as
being sensitive to the existing level of free assets, the retention level will be particularly sensitive
to the design of the product …                                                                         [½]
… and the expected new business volumes and mix.                                                          [½]
The results of modelling investigations will be crucial in order to find retention levels that
maximise profits subject to acceptable risk, and/or provide adequate protection of the insurer’s
free assets against new business strain for minimum cost.                                       [½]
The insurer might reinsure more than purely financial factors would suggest if it is looking to
obtain reinsurer’s assistance in certain areas, eg underwriting.                                          [½]
The retention level will also be affected by the cost / types of reinsurance available.              [½]
[Maximum 4]
Syllabus objectives: 3.3       Demonstrate the application of underwriting as a risk management technique.
## 3.3.1 Outline the purposes of underwr: 3.3.2 Describe the different approaches by which underwriting is applied.
3.3.3 Discuss the factors that should be considered when determining the level of
0 Introduction: Passing risks to a reinsurer is not the only way of mitigating and controlling a health and care
insurer’s risks. This chapter describes other methods to achieve this aim.
Before you read this chapter, you might want to remind yourself of the risks that an insurer faces,
by re‐reading the summaries of Chapters 2325.
1 Underwriting: Underwriting is the process of consideration of an insurance risk. This includes assessing
whether the risk is acceptable and, if so, setting the appropriate premium, together with the
Full medical underwriting: Full medical underwriting is the most onerous and detailed form of underwriting. It is
relatively costly and time-consuming, but gives the insurer the greatest opportunity to learn
about the individual’s state of health.
Here, the proposer is medically underwritten at the time of application and information on
medical history (and possibly other evidence) gathered. Some applicants will be accepted on
standard terms, while other policies will have special terms imposed, eg someone with a history
of back trouble may have ‘disorders of the back and associated conditions’ specifically excluded
Full medical underwriting is arguably clearer from a policyholder viewpoint, since they know from
the outset what is/is not covered by their policy.
Medical history disregarded (MHD): At the other extreme is medical history disregarded (MHD) underwriting, where there is no
consideration of the individual’s past medical history and no exclusions are made for
pre-existing medical conditions. Whilst less costly and time-consuming in terms of
process, this creates the greatest potential for anti-selection and so the product would have
It is most commonly used for group PMI business, or it may be offered to an individual on
transfer from a group to individual PMI policy.
Moratorium underwriting: Another alternative is moratorium underwriting. No formal underwriting is carried out at the
point of acceptance, but past medical history is examined at the time of claim.
There are two defined periods that are r:          The applicant can claim for any condition other than those pre-existing in a defined
period before acceptance. This is effectively an exclusion of all conditions that have
received treatment in a defined period prior to application to the insurer. This first
defined period is often five years.
         This exclusion is waived after a second defined period (usually set at two or three
years) if the policyholder receives no further treatment for the condition. It is this
period that gives rise to the name moratorium.
If the policyholder has not received any treatment for a condition that is excluded
under the moratorium during the two / three years after the policy was initially taken
out, that exclusion is removed. However, if the policyholder does receive treatment,
the moratorium exclusion period starts again, ie there must be a consecutive period
of two / three years without receiving treatment in order to be given cover for that
particular condition.
Moratorium underwriting is most suited to PMI because of the short-term nature of the
policy (typically one year at a time), and may also be used for group CI insurance. Due to
marketing considerations, the insurer can encourage purchase by offering the prospect of
immediate cover subject only to a total exclusion of all pre-existing conditions.
Relative to full medical underwriting, the moratorium process can encourage sales and
reduce new business costs. It has however been criticised because the policyholder cannot
be sure for what illnesses or treatments they are insured, unless they are sufficiently
medically aware to understand the connectivity of medical conditions.
The difficulty here is that the policy excludes not only recurrences of the pre‐existing conditions,
but also any conditions directly relating to them.
Question: Why is the cost per policy of moratorium underwriting much less than the cost per policy of full
medical underwriting?
Why will an insurer aim to accept most of its business at standard rates?
Why do policyholders not have to inform the insurer if their regular employment income rises?
What are the main advantages to the insurer of pre‐authorisation of claims?
List, giving examples, the main features of the design of a product that might be considered to
help control the risks to which an insurer is exposed.
(i)       List factors affecting expectations of a health insurer’s customers.
(ii)      List the aspects about which an insurer should ensure that its customers are being treated
fairly and in line with these expectations.
A critical illness policy with level regular premiums is sold for a term of 30 years, on fully
guaranteed terms. The policyholder lapses after 25 years. No surrender benefit is paid.
The experience of the insurer over the past 25 years has been, in aggregate, considerably worse
than expected when the policy was priced, and the result is that the policy now has a negative
asset share.
Discuss whether the insurer’s profitability is likely to have been adversely affected by this lapse.
Explain why health and care insurance claims have sometimes been paid even though they were
not valid as defined by the contract wording.
What areas should be covered in a contract between an insurer and a third party provider of
services?
Give an example of correlated risks that might reduce the overall risk to an insurer selling an IP
insurance contract.
Can you think of any risks for an insurance company that uses ‘big data’?
List areas of competence or expertise in claims management that will be needed by a health and
care insurer.
1.2 The process of full medical underw: The process of medical underwriting will vary by market practice, by product and possibly
by regulatory control. The following are some of the more usual steps to be found for
Medical and other evidence: Where the insurer is at risk on sickness (or death) under a contract, it will obtain evidence
about the health of applicants to assess if they have the insurer’s required standard of
health, and if not, how their health compares to that standard, ie what additional risk
 questions on the proposal form:          reports from medical doctors that the applicant has consulted, eg a primary medical
         a medical examination carried out on the applicant at the request of the insurer,
eg an examination undertaken by a doctor or nurse appointed by the insurance company
 specialist medical tests on th: The proposal form will ask for demographic information and include a series of questions about
the applicant’s medical history. The questions must be factual and not require technical
knowledge. For example, it is acceptable to ask when and why the applicant has been an
in‐patient in hospital, but not to ask if their heart attack was serious.
The last three sources will usually involve additional expense for the insurance company.
Hence the extent to which they are used in a particular case depends on the extent of the
loss that the insurer will make if it misestimates the state of health of the applicant.
The expected losses from limited underwriting will be greater for larger sums at risk. So the
decision to seek extra evidence will usually be limited to policies with a large sum at risk.
For example, for IP, the maximum sum at risk can be obtained by multiplying the weekly benefit
by the maximum number of weeks for which the benefit could be paid. Rules might then be used,
eg for sums at risk of up to $X, no additional underwriting would be carried out, for sums at risk
between $X and $Y, a PMAR would be obtained etc. Note that there might be a different set of
rules for applicants of different ages, with the cut‐off sums at risk being lower for older proposers.
The last three sources will also require the consent of the proposer. This is usually obtained when
Besides the state of health of the applicant, other factors that can affect the mortality or
sickness risk need to be investigated, namely any risks associated with:
 the leisure pursuits of the ap:          the applicant’s normal country of residence (and possibly also overseas travel).
To counter the risk of over-insurance, financial details of the applicant may be obtained.
Interpretation of the evidence: The evidence obtained needs to be interpreted in terms of the standard level of health
required by the insurer. This will be done by specialist underwriters employed by the
insurer, who will make use of:
         any doctors specifically employed by the insurer for this purpose
         underwriting manuals prepared internally or by the major reinsurance companies.
Underwriting decision: When an application is deemed to represent a higher risk than that assumed in the pricing
assumptions, the underwriter still has choices. The decision will be influenced by the
specific situation and by company philosophy.
Cases that are considered to bring highe: (a)       be offered higher (loaded) premiums or a lower benefit
(b)       be postponed (if the period of higher risk is deemed temporary)
(c)       be declined (if the additional risk is thought to be too high)
(d)       be offered a different type of policy (less risk intensive)
(e)       be offered to a reinsurer facultatively with zero retention
(f)       have certain specific causes and/or conditions excluded.
In the example there are several possibl:          to accept the proposer at standard premium rates and on standard policy terms
         to accept at standard rates, but with an extra policy condition restricting the cover offered
         to accept with a loading on the premium (+20% and +50% in the example below)
 to accept with the exclusion c:          to increase the policy excess (ie reduce the benefit) for claims related to this condition
Example: In the UK, the insurance industry body (the ABI) undertook consumer research to identify what
consumers would find most useful as an aid to understanding and comparing IP products. The
results of this research formed the basis for what has developed into a Statement of Best Practice
for the selling of IP. This outlines the sales process that must be followed and the information
that must be provided to the customer.
Similar statements are in force in the UK for the selling of CI, LTCI and PMI.
Bancassurers hold a large quantity of information about a customer who also has a bank
account with them, including data on that customer’s spending habits. The ability to
analyse and use such information to understand better the underlying risks will become
increasingly important in insurance, as more sophisticated analytical techniques develop to
The bank can analyse spending patterns to look for behaviour associated with low morbidity rates
(eg subscriptions to a gym) or high morbidity rates (eg purchases of potholing equipment). The
bank can then pass this information to its insurance operation.
Other insurance companies could access other sources of data. For example, supermarkets
collect large quantities of information through loyalty card schemes: customers that buy lots of
fresh fruit might have lower morbidity rates than customers that buy lots of cream buns.
Asthma and bronchial asthma: This is a disease characterised by airway inflammation and bronchospasm that results in airflow
obstruction causing dyspnea. Early and continued treatment with inhaled anti‐inflammatory
agents is the norm for most individuals with asthma. Asthma used to be classified as being
extrinsic (due to outside factors) or intrinsic (aetiology unknown). It is now felt that all asthma is
extrinsic in nature even though a cause may not be readily apparent. The causes of asthma are
often discussed in terms of triggers or inducers.
It used to be believed that the use of inhaled steroids was a sign of increased risk, however,
recent studies have shown that individuals who regularly use inhaled steroids appear to have
improved morbidity over those who do not. It should be noted however, that individuals who use
inhaled steroids on a regular basis do have a more severe form of asthma than those who only
Classification: Type 1              Bronchodilator use no more than once per week, no inhaled or oral steroids, no
Type 2              Bronchodilator or inhaled steroid use 1 – 2 times per day on a seasonal basis (5
times per year with durations not over one month), no hospitalisations in the past
Type 3              Bronchodilator use no more than 3 times per day, or inhaled steroid use no more
than 2 times per day, no oral steroids, no hospitalisations in past 2 years.
Type 4              Bronchodilator or inhaled steroid use up to 4 times per day on a year round basis,
no hospitalisations, no oral steroid use in past year.
Type 5              Oral or inhaled steroids more than above, hospitalisations, no oral steroid use in
Exclusion clause: Exclusion clause: Asthma, bronchitis, allergies, allergic rhinitis or reactive airway disease,
including any treatment or diagnostic evaluation.
* If age of onset over age 50, underwrite as Type 3
Smokers                                           Add 20% for Type 1; Decline others
Children under 2: Higher excess instead of exclusion clause         £1,000 Types 1 and 2 only
Medical underwriting can be used to mana:          It can protect an insurance company from anti-selection and in particular from lives
whose health is so seriously impaired that it is impossible to assess the risk
accurately. Anti-selection can lead to both higher than expected frequency of claims
         The medical underwriting process enables insurers to identify lives with a
substandard health risk for whom special terms must be quoted. A company may,
however, aim to write a large proportion of the business that it accepts at its
Relationship with product development an: The pricing actuary will establish a pricing structure against classes of risks. For example,
a price will be set for all critical illness insurance customers who are aged 34, who are
female, who are non-smokers and who have no adverse medical history.
The above is just one example – the actuary will, of course, also set prices for other homogenous
The actuary will use data from various sources, discussed in Chapter 12, to calculate this
price and will make any adjustments necessary to make the calculations consistent on
average with the categories of lives that will pay this price.
The general idea is that actuaries, given (hopefully) vast volumes of credible, reliable past data,
can use their models and expertise to put together a set of premium rates going forward, for
The actuary will rely on the underwriter, in processing the application forms, to accept lives
in accordance with these principles, ie that each policyholder should be charged a price
consistent with the risk undertaken. This will require the underwriter to have detailed
knowledge of the assumptions used by the actuary in gathering data and calculating prices,
in terms of the expected range of risks to be insured. The underwriter will also need to
demonstrate expertise in assessing the degree to which any individual proposer, in terms of
occupation, previous medical conditions, family history and other potential claim factors,
represents a risk within the boundaries of the pricing expectations.
The actuary and underwriter will therefore agree a set of guidelines within which a so‐called
A close working relationship between pricing actuary and underwriter is very important.
Frequently also the reinsurer is on hand to assist both in reaching a consistent structure for
The underwriters’ knowledge of new business procedures, their awareness of the risks
presented and their position at the front line of contact with distributor and/or customer,
also give them a significant contribution to make to the product development process.
Additionally, their involvement in the deliberation of policy conditions and in aspects of the
pricing will give them an understanding of the intentions of the policy and the risks that the
For example, underwriters could be involved in the development of a CI product at the stage
when the exact medical conditions covered are put into the policy wording. Their experience of
dealing with unusual conditions (for example, for the purpose of deciding exclusions) should put
them in a good position to know exactly how certain conditions and exclusions should be worded
Ongoing analysis: The actuary (and underwriter) will look very carefully at the actual level of new business
against that expected. The data will be broken down, where possible and credible, into the
various homogeneous risk cells. The reason for any significant departure will be analysed
to see whether this is a feature of the new business acceptance process. Here the
experience of other companies operating in the same lines of business will be very
informative. The discovery of significant differences from expectation will require either a
change to procedures or a change to the pricing assumptions.
This is covered further in Chapter 29, which covers monitoring and feedback.
The actuary, after some years of experience has been accumulated, will also review the
claims accepted under business underwritten on special terms in comparison with those
accepted at standard terms, in order to judge the appropriateness of the loadings or other
adjustments applied. It may well be that reinsurers’ larger volume of experience can
provide valuable and credible input into this process.
This, in theory at least, becomes possible because as experience grows: what was once ‘non‐
standard’ in terms of proposer characteristics might become a credible volume of experience that
can be rated using standard actuarial techniques.
However, in practice, business accepted on non‐standard terms is typically very heterogeneous.
There may be enough data to get a feel for whether the level of loadings in the portfolio as a
whole is appropriate, but such an investigation would probably not help at all in investigating
individual rating terms.
It is hard to look at experience on individual rating terms because there is often not enough
properly coded data to do so. Even if in reality the experience is sufficient, then the data quality
may not be good enough to allow analysis.
2 Claims management: The underwriters decide at the proposal stage which risks are accepted, but just as important are
the claims managers who assess risks at the claim stage. Health and care products can have
complex claim conditions, and the skill of the claims management team will have a big influence
on the claims experience of the insurer.
2.1 Purpose: A key component of risk management lies in ensuring that the claims accepted are
consistent with the assumptions made when the product was designed and priced. To this
end, the claims management team should be involved in the process of product
development and there should be regular dialogue between the claims and pricing / design
teams.
Equally, there should be close liaison between underwriting and claims managers to ensure
that the medical conditions giving rise to a long-term care insurance claim, for example, are
consistent with the information provided at the proposal stage, ie that such health
impairments were not present at initial application, or were explicitly admitted.
The medical investigations required to substantiate a claim can sometimes contradict the
information provided when the policy was taken out. The insurer's decision will depend on
the facts of the discrepancy and whether there have been attempts to act fraudulently or
withhold information.
Every large claim should be investigated, and the information at proposal verified to ensure that
the claim is not fraudulent or subject to non‐disclosure. For example, following an IP claim, a
policyholder’s medical practitioner may be contacted to confirm that the cause of the current
illness was not an undisclosed pre‐existing condition at the time of the policy’s inception.
Specific claims management techniques are found in particular health and care insurance
lines.
The following two sections look at techniques that are specific to IP insurance and PMI.
Replacement ratios: Under income protection insurance, it is deemed necessary to provide a benefit at such a
level that the claimant has an incentive to return to work. Thus, in many territories, insurers
impose policy conditions limiting the amount of benefit relative to salary at time of
disability. These limits, the maximum benefit at claim, are typically expressed as a
There may also be an offset for State disability benefits or other forms of continuing
income. Examples of maximum benefit formulae include 50% gross salary and 75% gross
salary less the single person’s State disability benefit.
If a policyholder’s regular employment income falls, there is an incentive to contact the
insurer and to review the level of cover and so reduce the premium paid. If this does not
happen then the application of these limits at the claim stage will result in client
dissatisfaction at a time of ill-health, when the benefit for which premiums have been paid is
Disability counselling: Also under income protection insurance, insurers in some territories will often use disability
counselling services at the time at which a claim is accepted. This entails a visit to the
home of the policyholder at the time of commencement of claim payment, by a specialist
representative of the insurer or of a third party support service. This process is often
welcomed by the claimant in that it can assist with information on State welfare payments
and local assistance services. From the insurer’s point of view, it can help to establish the
severity of the illness and a likely date for a return to work.
Another advantage of the insurer getting more actively involved at the claim stage like this, is to
promote the idea of returning to work as soon as is practically and reasonably possible. This
keeps claims costs to a minimum.
Pre‐authorisation: Under private medical insurance, the insurer wants to confirm that the proposed treatment
is appropriate and may not wish to rely on the claimant’s personal doctor as the only source
of information for the medical need of such treatment and its manner of delivery.
Some insurers have therefore put pre-authorisation procedures in place. By such means,
claims can be authenticated against the conditions in the policy and medical need can be
Further, the process can attempt to direct the patient into a service provider or hospital that
will be best equipped to handle the treatment (at a cost acceptable to the insurer). In some
territories, pre-authorisation of claims has not been widely accepted by customers because
it is deemed to infringe on their perceived rights to receive treatment where and when they
Under pre‐authorisation, when a policyholder becomes aware of the need to claim (eg when the
GP makes a referral for treatment), the policyholder should call the insurer to advise it of the
condition, discuss the proposed treatment and check whether it is covered under the policy.
3 Checks on policy and claims data: Data checks, such as reconciliation over time and internal consistency of items such as
sums insured and premiums, are described in Subject CP1. The following highlights some
of the areas relating to data checks that are of specific concern to a health and care insurer.
3.1 Recording accuracy: The accuracy of policy and claims data is a major risk for any insurer. Policy data record
necessary information for administration and client contact, while claims data record the
necessary information for benefit payout. In combination, both also provide the basis for
policy experience against which future business is priced and current risks are reserved. It
is vital for experience analysis that the claims data are accurate. Part of the internal and
external audit functions of the insurance operation will be to consider the reliability of the
information on which the business is conducted.
Ensuring that the proposal form and administration system input screens have the same
format will help to reduce the risk of error.
3.2 Regular vetting and spot checks: The auditing of the accuracy of information will entail the regular inspection of the
processes by which data are accepted by the system, as well as considering whether the
data captured are comprehensive. This will involve a systematic comparison of paper
records (and electronic records if telephone underwriting is used) on a periodic basis
against the facts stored, and deviations noted. It is also advisable that the systems that
process the information should have modules that query any possible inconsistency or
Policy records should be checked ‘end to end’, ie all the way through the data process from
input to eventual use, particularly if the data is passed between several systems and/or
3.3 Controls on data acceptance: The software for accepting data input (eg policyholder details, claims details, distributor
details) should have inbuilt checks that prevent invalid items from being accepted.
Examples of this would be a gender field other than M or F, an age at entry in excess of 120,
a sum insured in pence (or cents or other trivial amount). The features of the product itself
will impose further restrictions on the data acceptable.
As well as looking at individual values, it may also be possible to group items and look at how well
distributed they are. For example, an unusually high clustering of birth month may represent a
data input error worthy of further investigation.
Certain ‘errors’ may have exceptions that can only be overwritten by persons of a pre-
specified status within the organisation. An audit trail of such special exceptions will be
stored by the system. It may be that the company has agreed, on a one-off basis, to offer a
non-standard policy to a particular customer. This may need special permission as well as
3.4 Compulsory fields: An individual policy record will have certain fields that are mandatory; the input will not be
accepted unless all such information is included. Typical of these are age, gender and
benefit. A claim record will not be accepted unless there is a policy number for cross-
As a minimum, all the information that is used to price the policy (ie the rating factors) will be
 establish a culture of the val:          develop the ability to spot information that may have been submitted wrongly (either
 ensure that data are input spe: There will be a close liaison between the staff responsible for establishing the software to
accept the new data and the staff involved in the training. Feedback from the staff
responsible for data input should be encouraged.
Staff really need to understand how the data they are inputting are used and the implications to
the business if the data are wrong. This means that an open and trusting relationship should be
established between the inputting staff and the users of the data.
4 Product design: Product design has been considered in earlier chapters and forms a key element of risk
Design decisions such as the form of benefits, claim definitions and the inclusion of
guarantees and options have a direct impact on the level and type of risk to which the
Appropriateness of design for the chosen target market is also relevant (see Section 5), for
example in relation to new business volume, mis-selling and lapse risks.
Another important element of risk management is the terms and conditions, particularly
ensuing that they are clearly worded and consistent with pricing assumptions.
The following features of the design of:          the form of benefits, eg cash vs indemnity benefits for a LTCI contract
         claim definitions, eg own occupation vs any occupation claim definitions for IP contracts
         the inclusion of guarantees and options, eg GIOs for a CI contract
         the appropriateness of the design for the chosen target market, eg new business volumes
5.1 Distribution: The salesperson, whether employed or independent, is often the first contact between the
Continued customer satisfaction relies upon the customer having been sold products that
meet needs at a price that the policyholder regards as reasonable and affordable.
Aggressive salespeople may attempt, in the short term, to boost their own commission but
will endanger the whole client relationship, when at some future date the customers review
their finances and identify that the coverage is surplus to needs. In these circumstances
the likelihood is a total policy lapse, rather than a reduction in its scope, with subsequent
It is also possible that this one bad experience will cause policyholders to lapse other policies held
with the insurer. Policyholders may discuss their experiences with their partner, friends or
colleagues and encourage them either not to deal with this insurer or to lapse their existing
policies too. Bad news spreads fast.
Monitor the sales message:          Promises made should be consistent with the conditions in the insurance contract.
         Literature to support the product and its sale should be customer friendly, clear and
Beware business churning:          Salespeople should not be encouraging policyholders to lapse policies with a view
to taking out others (with the same or a different insurer) and thus undergoing a
         Products should offer value when measured against new business terms.
 There should be an adequate pr: Recall that commission clawback is where, in the event of a policy lapsing, part of the
commission already paid to the salesperson or intermediary is paid back to the insurer.
This discourages mis‐selling and helps to ensure that the customer’s needs are met.
         There should be an appropriate balance between initial and ongoing commission (or
Analyse the quality of sales staff:          The record of sales agents should be analysed for volumes written and for
         Complaints against (and compliments about) each agent should be reviewed.
Capturing this information means that it is essential that an insurer’s data systems also
capture the ‘agent code’ on each sale.
 Commission should be commensur: If the two are not consistent, the intermediary will simply sell the products that are
quickest and easiest to sell, thus maximising the commission received. This may result in
a business mix that is not compatible with expectations.
In other words, the loading for commission in the premium should be consistent with that
 Commission should not encourag:          Commission should be matched with clawback controls on early lapse.
Monitor premium receipts:          Premiums should be received from agents / brokers in a timely manner.
         Policies should not be issued without evidence of premium receipt.
In addition, premiums should be received in a timely manner as the insurer cannot earn
investment income on premiums being held by brokers.
Invest in sales training:          Salespeople and sales support staff should be adequately trained on sales
processes, including the need to obtain robust information on customers’ health and
care insurance needs and their ability to pay.
Many of these sales processes may be recommended or imposed by regulatory or
         Salespeople and sales support staff should be adequately trained on products and
acceptance procedures, such as medical underwriting procedures.
5.2 Treating customers fairly (TCF): Many regulators impose additional requirements on insurers to ensure that consumers are
treated fairly. These additional requirements can impact on the whole process for
managing the customer relationship, as well as including practical requirements such as
additional literature to be supplied as part of the sales process, and in terms of the claims
payable in the claims process. Often there will be generally accepted features that products
of a particular type should include.
 benefits paid in the past: (ii)      Aspects about which customers should be treated fairly
 period of cover and guarantees:          reviewability conditions (ie if premiums and/or benefits are reviewable)
 ability to make amendments (eg:          security of financial transaction made (eg if sold over the internet) and data protection
Any insurer that does not ensure its customers are being treated fairly in all aspects of its business
runs the risk that the regulator will take action against it for not meeting its statutory
requirements. For example, it may impose fines or restrictions on the insurer. Even in countries
with no such regulations, policyholders are more likely to lapse (or complain) if they feel they are
The risk of not treating customers fairly can be mitigated in several ways.
The insurer should avoid promising more than the scope of the product in such ways,
making all product conditions clear and explicit, and monitoring sales processes
This monitoring should ensure that customers’ expectations are being kept in line with what the
5.3 Surveys on customer service satisf: In an age where customer sophistication is growing, there are many more considerations other
than price alone, when buying a health product.
Understanding what has been bought: A customer buys an insurance product expecting it to provide a service, ie to cover a
certain aspect of health and care risks that might befall the customer after the insurance is
purchased. Much investment in terms of time and money has been made in the process of
purchase and sale; thus it is imperative that both insurer and insured share the same
understanding of what the product entails.
The risk of misunderstanding is great. There is the wastage of that investment plus the
potential for reputational damage and the loss of existing or future business from a
customer. In an extreme circumstance, regulators could enforce the insurer to meet the
policyholder’s expectations (against the insurer’s own intentions) where the product was
mis-sold or other literature was misleading.
This is not as uncommon as you might expect (or hope). In recent years, there have been many
examples of mis‐selling, although most of the concerns have been in life and pensions business
rather than in health and care. As a result, regulators are continually reviewing legislation in the
hope that this can be prevented from happening in the future.
A survey of client understanding can serve the purpose of ensuring policyholder
satisfaction with both the sales process and the product itself. Results of this survey,
performed as part of an after-sales service, should be fed back into the literature provided
and into the routines by which products are explained and sold.
Meeting needs: The best way to ensure customer satisfaction is to provide a product that meets the needs
that the person has identified as providing grounds for insurance protection. The more
artificial the needs creation through the sales process, the less likely the policyholder is to
maintain the product itself, let alone purchase further insurance policies from the insurer.
Meeting customer needs was discussed in: If policyholder needs are not met, there is a strong chance that the policy will lapse. Lapses can
be a source of loss for insurers, depending on the type of product and the precise benefits on
lapsing. At the very least, an earlier‐than‐expected lapse may cause problems if the initial
expenses have yet to be recouped.
Information and literature: The customer will expect to receive information on a regular basis as promised. This may
 contract renewal details under: Further information, if appropriate and timely, may enhance the client relationship.
However, any excessive attempts to cross-sell products could incur customer
dissatisfaction and may result in early termination of the existing policy.
Claims payment satisfaction: The customer will expect efficient payment of claims amounts in line with product promises.
There is a balance to be drawn here between the need for sufficient information to verify the
entitlement to claim and the client’s expectations at a time when there is a breakdown in
health and a need for financial compensation.
Implications: In most instances, the insurer has an expectation of future profit from existing customers.
The phrases ‘embedded value’ and ‘customer lifetime value’ are relevant here. The insurer
runs the risk of losing much of this asset if customer satisfaction is not a priority of the
whole operation. If policyholders lapse their policies due to dissatisfaction, the insurer is
deprived not only of the future margins in the existing contract, but also, probably, of the
You have met the concept of embedded values in Chapters 13 and 21, and you will meet it again
Customer lifetime value is a concept used within any business. It is an attempt to quantify the
profit that a customer is expected to bring to the insurer, taking into account the entire future
lifetime of his needs and the extent of cross‐selling possible.
Other anecdotal evidence shows that whereas happy customers only tell one other person about
their experiences, unhappy customers will tell many more people about their experience. Poor
customer satisfaction therefore has a far bigger effect on profit (or losses).
6 Service level agreements with outs: As well as managing the relationship with distributors and with customers, it is important to
manage risks associated with other counterparties. Other counterparties range from reinsurers
to providers of medical services to providers of other services (such as administration or claims
 through due diligence on the c: Due diligence involves an investigation of the counterparty prior to signing a contract.
Performing such investigations should enable the insurer to make an informed decision
about whether the counterparty meets the required standards.
         restriction on the use of counterparties below a specified credit rating
This should help to ensure that counterparties meet certain standards. It may be a
quicker, simpler way of ascertaining the quality of outsourcers without carrying out a full
 diversification across differe: By using several counterparties, the insurer will reduce the risk of default / poor
performance by any single counterparty. If you have studied Subject CP1 (previously
Subject CA1), you may remember that insurers should diversify by provider of reinsurance
and this point generalises this idea to any counterparties the insurer uses.
Note that the insurer may be limited in its ability to diversify in certain circumstances, for
example, if it is small or if there is only a limited number of possible counterparties.
 single counterparty exposure l: This is similar to the point above about reducing the risk of default / poor performance by
 credit insurance or derivative: Credit insurance is a type of general insurance that should protect the insurer against the
risk of default, insolvency or bankruptcy of the counterparty. A credit derivative may be
used to transfer the risk of counterparty default to a third party.
6.2 Use of outsourcing: In many territories of the world, third party specialists have been developed to assist the
insurer in several core areas of business processes. The third party salesperson has been
around for a long time and is characterised by the insurance intermediary or broker.
Equally companies have been set up that: underwriting, claims management, actuarial functions (for example, pricing and reserving),
administration, investment, marketing, systems and training.
The key offering is that the third party provides a level of expertise and efficiency at a price
that may be cheaper than in-house costs, and leaves the insurer’s management to perform
the other tasks where they feel most competent, including, of course, the strategic
6.3 Service level agreements: Once the third party is chosen, a service level agreement will be put in place.
This will be a legal contract, and is often known as an SLA (Service Level Agreement). The SLA
should contain precise details of the role that each party agrees to undertake (very much like a
reinsurance treaty). The details depend on what each party agrees and is prepared to accept, and
often problems involving third parties are due to imprecise or ambiguous SLAs.
In return for fees (unit costs or periodic payments), certain tasks will be performed to a
pre-specified standard and within pre-specified times. The insurer should be confident that
by passing certain processes in the business chain to experts, the insurer is reducing risks.
However, since the tasks are being performed by third parties, the insurer will need to have
regular reports, checks and inspections to guard against reputational risks, business risks
and security issues. Timely resolution or correction of any issues will also be required.
For example, a third party performing the underwriting function on behalf of an insurer will need
to report on, and meet certain required targets in respect of:
 dates for which the agreement:          who the parties are, who the main contacts are, who is going to carry out the work and
who the ultimate responsibility lies with
         what the third party will provide, including to what level and at what frequency
         payments to be made to the third party (amounts, frequency, methods, conditions etc)
 any other obligations on the i:          how, and how often, the standard of service will be monitored
         what action will be taken if service standards are failed (eg penalties)
7.1 Enterprise risk management: Enterprise risk management (ERM) is a risk management framework that considers the risks
of the business as a whole, rather than considering individual risks in isolation. This allows
the concentration of risk arising from a variety of sources within an organisation to be
appreciated, and for the diversifying effects of risks to be allowed for. This is the holistic,
You may have read about enterprise risk management in earlier subjects, such as Subject CP1
An insurer needs to be aware of, and assess, the overall risk profile to which it is exposed,
based on an aggregation of the underlying risks that it faces, allowing for correlating
Correlations may have both positive and negative effects. Positive correlations between risks will
typically act to increase the risk, whereas negative correlations will typically act to reduce the risk.
Therefore claim inception rates and longevity are likely to be negatively correlated.
Reduced claim inception rates will be a good thing for the insurer as there will be fewer IP claims.
Increased longevity may be a bad thing for the insurer as claims could last longer. Therefore the
combination of reduced claim inception rates and increased longevity should be less bad for the
The role of the actuary in managing an insurer’s risks should therefore be a regular
programme of advising the directors of the nature and size of the risks faced. Particular
attention will need to be given to those risks that are the most material and/or the most
Risks should be controlled by analysing and explaining their nature, costing them as far as
possible, and agreeing on management strategies for them. In the case of sensitive risks,
the actuary should model a range of long-term scenarios to show the impact of variations to
future experience, and management strategies should be designed to protect against those
risks that the insurer is able to (and chooses to) control.
Once risk management strategies have been agreed, they should be documented and
implemented. Where the strategies consist of objective rules and procedures, these should
then be monitored on an ongoing basis.
If you have studied Subject CP1 (previously Subject CA1), you will be familiar with the process of
Strong governance and controls are vital to an insurer’s risk management framework.
Another element of ERM is the recognition that value can be added to a business through
educated risk-taking, with a strong risk management framework that better allows
companies to identify and assess strategic opportunities.
7.2 Experience monitoring and control: As part of the actuarial control cycle, the actuary will periodically review the experience of the
insurance company’s own portfolio of health and care insurance business.
The periodic review of an insurer’s experience (including claims, expenses and distribution,
and analysis of surplus) is considered in more detail in Chapter 29.
Such analyses can help the insurer to identify the appropriate risk management actions,
such as described elsewhere in this chapter (and Chapter 26) or other actions such as:
7.3 Data analytics and predictive mode: Relating to the above section, health and care insurers are able to take advantage of
technical developments in the analysis of large volumes of data (‘big data’).
In general, big data is a broad term for data sets that are so large or complex that traditional data
Increasingly large quantities of data are being recorded about individuals every day: the things
they buy, the places they go, the websites they visit. As computers get ever faster, it is possible to
analyse these data to look for patterns and predict future behaviour. For example, some internet
stores are incredibly good at predicting the goods and services people want and sending
personalised adverts for just the right thing.
 to aid prediction, eg through: Risk classifications are likely to develop as a result, allowing more accurate rating for each
