individual customer. The insurer also then has greater ability to select the preferred risks.
Traditionally, health and care insurance has been rated on factors such as age, gender, occupation
and location, and then making generalisations that all the people within a particular rating cell will
act in the same way. In the future, health and care insurance may be rated on factors that
directly affect morbidity such as diet and exercise.
Monitoring the available data may also allow the insurer to drive better experience, through
early identification of changes in individual insured risks or potentially through being able
to intervene and influence customer behaviours.
For example, if a customer’s records showed a decline in the purchase of fruit and vegetables
then the insurer could send out advice on healthy eating.
Multi-factor predictive modelling techniques (eg using generalised linear models) are also
increasingly being adopted. These techniques can combine the internally held customer
data with external drivers, allowing for correlations and interactions between them.
7.4 Quality of staff: The successful performance of a business is down in large measure to the quality of the
staff employed and their exercise of judgement. Thus there needs to be in place adequate
systems of identification of competence levels required at the various stages of business
administration, of recruitment, of staff training, allocation and review and of recognition of
the need for outside expertise. Motivation and reward are other key features here.
These systems need to monitor for fraudulent as well as incompetent behaviour.
Health and care insurers have to be particularly careful about staff competence, because there is
far more opportunity for fraud and judgement in health and care insurance than in, say, life
insurance. That is because, in life insurance, the benefits are generally paid on death. It takes an
extreme scenario to generate a false claim here (although that’s not to say it doesn’t happen) and
a typical claims underwriting process is simply to have proof of death (eg death certificate).
In health and care insurance, however, there is more scope for fraud, since claims can be more
readily exaggerated or falsified. Benefits fraud is common subject material for television
programmes in some counties, eg where someone who is claiming IP benefit and is meant to be
sick off work, is filmed surfing on holiday.
The main area is in claims assessment an:          initial assessment of validity of a claim, including collection of the required evidence
 assessment of suitable treatme:          knowledge of likely inflationary pressures on health claims costs
 knowledge of medical procedure:          ongoing assessment and management of the claim (for IP insurance, LTCI, PMI).
In addition, expertise will be required in the initial underwriting of policies.
Companies may perform an occasional audit of staff competence. ‘Time and motions’
analysis by independent experts may also indicate the need for staff reallocation and
You may have been involved in a time and motion study at work if you have been asked to record
exactly what tasks you have carried out, and how much time was spent on each one, over a
period of time. The results can indicate who does what in a company and for how long. Hence, it
can be the first stage in identifying where improvements can be made, but the results do depend
on the accuracy of the information recorded.
Underwriting: Underwriting can be used to mitigate risk. Medical underwriting might be full medical
underwriting, moratorium underwriting (which is common under PMI) or medical history
disregarded (which is most likely to be used for group business).
With full medical underwriting:          evidence (medical, lifestyle or financial) is collected on each risk in order to classify
the risks into homogeneous groups so that they can be priced reliably and in line
         higher risks can be dealt with, eg premium loading, exclusion clause etc
 new business acceptances can b:          there must be a close link between underwriters and product developers.
With moratorium underwriting:          no formal underwriting is carried out at the point of acceptance
 past medical history is examin:          the applicant can claim for any condition other than those that were pre‐existing in a
defined period before acceptance (often five years)
         this exclusion is waived after a period of time – the moratorium period (usually two
or three years) – if the policyholder receives no further treatment for the condition
         if the policyholder does receive treatment for pre‐existing conditions, then the
Product‐specific claims controls:          for IP insurance, include insisting on notice of income reductions, and using disability
Policy and claims data should be checked:          to ensure the proposal form and administration systems have the same format
Product design: Risks can be controlled by considering the design of the product, in particular, the form of
benefits, the claim definition, the inclusion of guarantees and options and the terms and
conditions included in the policy wording.
The appropriateness of the product design for the chosen target market is also important.
Treating customers fairly (TCF) should h:          make all conditions clear, explicit and in line with customers’ expectations
         monitor sales processes, ensuring sales staff do not oversell products.
Managing other counterparties: Third parties may be used to carry out various functions, and the insurer may be able to
benefit from their expertise. Service level agreements with outsourcers may be used to
ensure the quality of work carried out by outsourcers and to ensure that the agreement
genuinely does act to mitigate risks.
Other internal processes: Enterprise risk management is a risk management that considers the risks of the business as
a whole, and so allows for both concentrations of risk and diversification.
The actuary should be involved in assessing the nature and size of the risks faced, analysing
and costing them, and documenting and reporting them.
Experience monitoring is covered in detail in a later chapter. Further, insurers may be able to
analyse ‘big data’ in order to improve risk classification, predictive modelling and experience
